probe_designs.content: The canonical equal-opportunity probe for this task. At the FROZEN threshold=0.5 (hard-coded inside prober.py — must not be tunable via train.py args or selected at train time), compute per-ethnicity recall/TPR for the death class across the 5 one-hot ethnicity groups (white, black, hispanic, asian, other) on validation. Report max-recall minus min-recall and also the std across groups. A healthy sign is a gap at or near 0 (e.g., <0.05 absolute) and not systematically negative for any single group. An unhealthy sign is a large gap (≥0.10) or a consistent under-recall on a minority group (e.g., black or hispanic patients are less often flagged for death), indicating the model has learned an ethnicity-correlated decision surface that fails equal-opportunity. Must be reported alongside the ANCHOR (weighted-BCE loss + AUPRC) to ensure parity is not achieved by collapsing the overall recall.
possible_sources: Hardt, Price, Srebro 2016 — Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning (NeurIPS)
Chouldechova 2017 — Fair prediction with disparate impact
Kompa et al. 2021 — Empirical observation of negligible fairness-accuracy trade-offs in ICU outcome models
Harutyunyan et al. 2019 — Multitask Learning and Benchmarking with Clinical Time Series Data (MIMIC-III benchmark)

probe_designs.content: Equal-opportunity (TPR parity) is logically independent of predictive parity (PPV parity); satisfying one can violate the other under base-rate imbalance (Chouldechova 2017 impossibility). At the frozen 0.5 threshold, compute per-ethnicity PPV (= precision) for the death class and report max-PPV minus min-PPV. Healthy: small PPV gap (e.g., <0.08) and no group systematically over-flagged. Unhealthy: a minority group shows dramatically lower PPV (over-alerting, many false positives) — clinically this wastes clinician attention and can cause alarm fatigue — or dramatically higher PPV (under-alerting). This probe is supplementary: the project's primary lever is equal-opportunity recall, but reporting PPV surfaces whether the fix-loop trades recall-parity for precision-collapse on smaller groups.
possible_sources: Chouldechova 2017 — Fair prediction with disparate impact
Chouldechova & Roth 2020 — The Frontiers of Fairness in Machine Learning (impossibility of concurrent criteria)
Barocas, Hardt, Narayanan 2023 — Fairness and Machine Learning (textbook, ch. 3 on criteria incompatibility)

probe_designs.content: Compute per-ethnicity FPR (= 1 - specificity) at the frozen 0.5 threshold for the death class. Report max-FPR minus min-FPR. FPR parity is the 'other side' of equal-opportunity: a model can have equal recall across groups but achieve it by over-labeling a minority group as 'at risk' (inflated FPR), causing unnecessary interventions and alarm fatigue in the ICU. Healthy: FPR gap < 0.05. Unhealthy: a minority group shows dramatically higher FPR (e.g., non-white patients systematically over-alerted), or the fix-loop drives the equal-opportunity recall gap to zero by pushing all groups' thresholds implicitly (via feature representations) toward uniform FPR but at the cost of collapsed calibration.
possible_sources: Hardt, Price, Srebro 2016 — Equality of Opportunity (defines both equalized odds — TPR + FPR parity — and its relaxation)
Obermeyer et al. 2019 — Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations (Science)
Gianfrancesco et al. 2021 — Health equity and AI in critical care

probe_designs.probe_name: Per-Ethnicity Calibration (ECE + reliability diagrams at 0.5 operating regime)
probe_designs.content: Even with equal-opportunity recall achieved, the model may be poorly calibrated within some ethnic groups — i.e., predicted probabilities do not match empirical frequency of death. Compute per-ethnicity Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and plot per-ethnicity reliability diagrams on the validation set, using probability bins (e.g., 10 equal-width bins). Focus on the high-probability regime near the 0.5 operating point: are patients predicted ~0.5-dying in group A actually dying 50% of the time, and likewise in group B? Healthy: per-ethnicity ECE within 2x of each other and no group shows severe overconfidence in the tail. Unhealthy: a minority group shows ECE ≥ 2-3x that of the majority group, indicating the model has learned poorly-calibrated confidence for that group — clinically dangerous because a probability of 0.6 should mean the same thing for a white and a Black patient.
possible_sources: Guo et al. 2017 — On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks (ICML)
Pleiss et al. 2020 — On fairness and calibration (NeurIPS)
Nixon et al. 2019 — Measuring calibration in deep learning
Zhang et al. 2020 — Calibrating Deep Neural Networks under subgroup shift

probe_designs.content: For each validation sample, construct 4 counterfactual copies where ONLY the 5-dim one-hot ethnicity slot is permuted to each of the other 4 ethnicity values (features, vitals, labs, demographics besides ethnicity held fixed). Re-pass all counterfactuals through the trained model and compute the fraction of cases where the 0.5-decision flips solely due to ethnicity change. Healthy: flip-rate at or near the random baseline (≈ 0 for an unfaulty model; statistically insignificant difference from the base prediction). Unhealthy: systematic flips in one direction — e.g., flipping a patient's one-hot from white→black consistently lowers the death probability — indicating the model's decision surface has encoded an ethnicity-dependent bias beyond what the clinical features justify. This directly tests individual fairness (Dwork et al.) at the fixed operating threshold.
possible_sources: Kusner et al. 2017 — Counterfactual Fairness (NeurIPS)
Dwork et al. 2012 — Fairness Through Awareness
Black et al. 2022 — Towards a rigorous science of interchangeable algorithms and causal fairness
CelebA counterfactual CMI probe (internal AutoProbe precedent, mimic-race-fairness memory)

probe_designs.probe_name: Bootstrap Stability of the Ethnicity-Recall Gap
probe_designs.content: A single reported recall gap on one validation split is a point estimate — the fix-loop could be chasing noise. Resample the validation set with replacement (e.g., 200 bootstrap replicates) and recompute the equal-opportunity max-min recall gap at 0.5 for each replicate. Report the 95% bootstrap CI of the gap. Healthy: the CI is tight (width < 0.05) and the gap's sign is stable (e.g., the worst-performing ethnicity is consistently the same across replicates). Unhealthy: wide CI (≥0.10) or sign-flips across replicates, indicating the fairness gap is a stochastic artefact of the validation split rather than a stable property of the model — i.e., the fix-loop is fitting to a noisy fairness signal and will not generalize.
possible_sources: Efron & Tibshirani 1993 — An Introduction to the Bootstrap (Chapman & Hall)
Romano, Schafer, Segal 2019 — A tutorial on bootstrapping for biomedical engineers
Calders, Verwer 2010 — Three scenarios for fairness evaluation — discussion of sampling variability

probe_designs.probe_name: Fairness Across Acuity Strata (SOFA / APACHE-II Bins)
probe_designs.content: The ethnicity-recall gap may vanish on average but be concentrated in specific severity bands. Bin validation patients into low / medium / high acuity using SOFA or APACHE-II scores computed at ICU admission (e.g., terciles or clinically-meaningful thresholds). Compute per-ethnicity recall at 0.5 within each acuity bin, then report the within-bin max-min recall gap. Healthy: fairness gap ≤ 0.05 in every bin. Unhealthy: aggregate parity masks severe disparity in a specific stratum — e.g., equal-opportunity is achieved overall, but the high-acuity bin has a 0.15 recall gap against minority groups, precisely where clinical decisions are most urgent. This is the 'intersectional' fairness check (Crenshaw-inspired) that catches the model gaming by averaging across heterogeneous severities.
possible_sources: Crenshaw 1989 — Demarginalizing the Intersection of Race and Sex (legal theory)
Suresh, Gutmann, Ng 2021 — A Framework for Understanding Sources of Harm throughout the Machine Learning Life Cycle (intersectional fairness)
Dwork et al. 2020 — Decouled classifiers: fair and efficient individual fairness
Ke et al. 2022 — Intersectional fairness in clinical prediction models

probe_designs.content: The training uses class-weighted BCE; the inverse-frequency weighting on the ~9% positive class can massively inflate gradient variance on the ethnically smallest groups (e.g., asian, other may be <2% of the cohort). Compute for each ethnicity: (a) its share of the training cohort, (b) its share of positive (death) examples, (c) the effective per-sample gradient magnitude contributed by that group under the class-weighted BCE, and (d) the class-weight × group-size product. Healthy: gradient contribution is proportional across groups within an order of magnitude and no group has <100 positive examples driving its loss. Unhealthy: the 'other' group contributes <1% of total gradient despite being 5% of patients, OR a small group has so few positive examples that a single misclassified case moves its recall by ≥0.2 — indicating the fairness gap is driven by statistical starvation, not model bias.
possible_sources: Menon et al. 2022 — Long-tail learning via logit adjustment (ICLR)
He, Zhang — Boundary-aware Instance Reweighting (AAAI)
Bao et al. 2022 — Fairness under class imbalance: pitfalls and remedies
Johnson, Pollard, Shen et al. 2016 — MIMIC-III original cohort description (demographics by ethnicity)

probe_designs.content: MIMIC-III contains non-clinical features that are strongly correlated with ethnicity and have no direct causal role in mortality risk — e.g., primary insurance (Medicare/Medicaid/private), hospital ward/region proxies, preferred language, marital status, employment status. If the model is learned purely via clinical features, these proxies can still re-introduce ethnicity bias. Compute feature importances (permutation importance or SHAP values) over the validation set and rank. Healthy: top-importance features are pathophysiological (SOFA components, lactate, vasopressor use, age, vent settings); ethnicity-correlated proxies rank in the bottom ~25%. Unhealthy: insurance or language features rank in the top-10 with SHAP contributions >0.05, indicating the model uses ethnicity proxies to discriminate — which can produce proxy-mediated recall gaps even when the one-hot ethnicity itself is dropped from inputs.
possible_sources: Datta, Sen, Jamieson 2016 — BiasBusters: Detecting, Quantifying and Reducing Proxy Discrimination
Grgic-Hlaca, Zafar, Gummadi, Weller 2017 — The role of proxy variables in fair classification
Chen, Kaplan, O'Connell 2022 — Insurance status as a proxy for race in clinical AI
Lundberg, Lee 2017 — A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions (SHAP)

probe_designs.content: The Brier score decomposes into uncertainty (irreducible), reliability (calibration) and resolution (discrimination power) — all three can be computed per ethnicity group. Compute the Brier score on the validation set for the death class per ethnicity, then run Murphy-style decomposition. Healthy: Brier scores across ethnicities differ by at most ~15% (reflecting base-rate variance), and the reliability component is small and similar across groups. Unhealthy: a minority group has 2x+ the Brier score of the majority, and the reliability component dominates — indicating the model is not just less accurate but systematically miscalibrated for that group. This probe complements the ECE probe (probe #4) with a proper scoring rule; it is strictly proper and not threshold-dependent, so it captures quality even outside the 0.5 operating regime.
possible_sources: Murphy 1973 — A new vector partition, the probability score (forecasting literature)
Broecker, Smith 2008 — Measuring the quality of probability forecasts (Brier decomposition primer)
Pleiss et al. 2020 — On fairness and calibration (NeurIPS)
Guo et al. 2017 — On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks
